source_file: Original draft for FlutterCon USA 2026 roundtable
question: Q3: Specialize Deeply or Stay Adaptable?

sections.prompt: Who here has changed what they're learning or how they learn because of AI?
sections.hint: Examples: moving across platforms, using AI to bridge gaps, learning product domains, and being able to build something useful even when the job market shifts.
no_hands: Why do you think deep specialization becomes more valuable with AI?
Which specialties are hardest for AI to flatten?
What risks come from being too broad?
hands_up: What kinds of adaptability matter most: technical, product, communication, or domain learning?
How does Flutter support an adaptable career path?
Where can broad developers still get exposed by lack of depth?
How do you decide when to go deeper versus wider?
follow_ups: What is one deep skill every adaptable Flutter developer still needs?
How does AI actually change the effectiveness of a generalist?
How do teams evaluate breadth during hiring?

sections.prompt: Who actively spends time on skills outside Flutter — backend, native, design, AI tooling, anything?
sections.hint: Examples: Flutter as a deep skill, with breadth across native mobile, backend, AI tooling, design, product, deployment, and analytics.
no_hands: How useful is the T-shaped idea when you're actually deciding what to learn?
How do you think about the tradeoff between going really deep on one thing versus spreading out?
What would you choose as your vertical skill instead of Flutter?
hands_up: What should be the deep vertical for a Flutter developer?
Which skills outside of Flutter feel most valuable to pair with it right now?
How do you avoid spreading yourself too thin?
What is a realistic T-shaped learning plan for six months?
follow_ups: Where does AI tooling fit in a developer's skill profile?
How do you show T-shaped ability in a portfolio?
What is one skill pairing with Flutter that looks underrated?

sections.prompt: By a show of hands, is a basic CRUD app still enough for a Flutter portfolio?
sections.hint: Examples: showing product reasoning, polish, distribution, metrics, maintainability, native integration, and real users instead of only generated screens.
no_hands: How do you see traditional portfolios holding up when the projects behind them are genuinely thoughtful?
What makes a simple Flutter app still impressive?
How should beginners prove skill without overbuilding?
hands_up: What makes a Flutter portfolio stand out when everyone can generate a decent-looking app shell?
What parts of the building process are worth showing alongside the finished app?
How important is shipping to stores or real users?
What project would prove both Flutter skill and career resilience?
follow_ups: What portfolio artifact shows judgment, not just code generation?
How should students talk about AI use in projects?
How do you weigh depth versus breadth when you're reviewing someone's portfolio?

sections.prompt: Has your learning routine actually changed in the past year or so?
sections.hint: Examples: less syntax memorization, more architecture, product, debugging, review, testing, release, and AI-assisted workflows.
no_hands: Why has your learning plan stayed the same?
Which old-school learning habits are still worth protecting?
What should learners avoid outsourcing to AI too early?
hands_up: What do you spend less time learning now?
What do you spend more time practicing now?
How do you learn a framework when AI can answer questions instantly?
What would you put in a weekly learning routine for Flutter plus AI?
follow_ups: How much time should go to building versus reading versus debugging?
What is the right role for official docs now?
How do you keep from becoming dependent on AI explanations?

wrap_up: What career shape would you honestly recommend to someone starting out today?
What is the most valuable skill to pair with Flutter?
What learning habit should developers protect as AI tools get better?
